student_profile.student_background: Graduate-level Computer Science students with diverse disciplinary and international backgrounds.
student_profile.aggregate_academic_performance: Generally strong academic readiness with varied prior exposure to ML and assessment styles.
student_profile.anticipated_learner_needs_and_barriers: Familiar with Python; uneven experience with tools like Colab; some gaps in math background; benefits from simplified language and visuals.

instructor_preferences.instructor_emphasis_intent:
Tools & Platforms to be used in class: Open‑source frameworks such as LangChain, LlamaIndex, and AutoGen for agent construction.
Benchmarks including AgentBench, τ‑bench, AceBench, MedAgentBench, ST‑WebAgentBench, and Agent‑SafetyBench for evaluation.
Vector database and SQL technologies (e.g., PostgreSQL with pgvector, Milvus) for memory and retrieval.
Safety evaluation and red‑teaming tools for prompt injection testing, hallucination control, and privacy auditing.
instructor_preferences.instructor_style_preferences: Structured instructional scripts, minimal slide clutter, and a professional but supportive tone.
instructor_preferences.instructor_focus_for_assessment: Project-driven assessment with open-ended tasks; traditional exams are not emphasized.

course_structure.course_learning_outcomes: Critically analyze and compare agent benchmarks and evaluation protocols; Explain how world models and memory architectures influence agent behavior and planning; Evaluate instruction‑following and planning capabilities of LLM‑based agents using recent techniques; Apply context engineering methods—prompt design, retrieval augmentation, and structured inputs—to improve performance; Assess and mitigate safety, privacy, and trustworthiness risks in LLM‑based agents; Build and evaluate a small agentic system for a real‑world task and present findings.
course_structure.weekly_schedule_outline:
Introduction to LLM‑based Agents — taxonomy of agents, safety as a design principle
Architectures & Building Blocks — planning loops, memory, action spaces, tool invocation
Agent Benchmarks I — QA vs. interactive tasks; AgentBench, TauBench, AceBench
Agent Benchmarks II — domain‑specific (clinical, code, embodied); evaluation protocols
World Models & State Representation — open‑world vs. closed‑world assumptions; internal vs. external state
Memory & Long‑Term Context — episodic vs. semantic memory, vector DBs, scratchpads
Instruction Following & Policy Learning — measuring reliability, reward hacking, RLHF and post‑training methods
Context Engineering I — prompt reformulation, few‑shot orchestration, structured prompts 
Context Engineering II — Retrieval‑Augmented Generation (RAG) vs. Long‑Context; hybrid symbolic–neural retrieval
Multi‑Agent Systems & Collaboration — orchestrators, role assignment, emergent behavior
Trustworthiness, Safety & Privacy — red‑teaming, hallucination control, prompt injection defense, auditable reasoning
Applications Deep Dive — choose one domain (clinical, education, cybersecurity); Safety
Student Project Check‑Ins & Poster Session — peer feedback on prototypes (including safety metrics)
Future Directions & Course Wrap‑Up — meta‑agents, auto‑formalization, theorem provers, safety by design

assessment_design.assessment_format_preferences:
Test – Mid Term and Final (30%) 
Short Projects (30%) – Small coding tasks that include running benchmarks, adding memory modules, implementing safety mitigations, and evaluating instruction‑following.
Semester Project (30%) – Each student or pair builds or evaluates an LLM‑based agentic system, with a mandatory safety/trustworthiness component and final presentation.
Participation (10%) – Students lead one discussion or safety‑related demo, contribute to peer feedback, and engage in class discussions. Some Possible Initial Semester Projects
Implement a hybrid retrieval router that chooses between RAG and long‑context models based on task requirements.
Extend an existing agent benchmark with new tasks, including safety‑critical domains such as finance or healthcare.
Design a multi‑agent collaboration scenario (planning or debate) with an integrated safety monitor agent.
Investigate instruction‑following failures using RLHF and propose mitigations based on GRPO‑λ or DeepSeek‑R1 methods.
Develop a privacy‑aware context engineering pipeline for a sensitive domain using retrieval‑augmented generation.
assessment_design.assessment_delivery_constraints: Submissions via learning management system in standard file formats (e.g., PDF, notebook).

teaching_constraints.platform_policy_constraints: Hosted on institution-approved LMS; materials must meet accessibility and compliance standards.
teaching_constraints.ta_support_availability: Part-time teaching assistant available for grading and support.
teaching_constraints.instructional_delivery_context: In-person sessions with live walkthroughs and laptop-based activities.

institutional_requirements.program_learning_outcomes: Aligns with learning goals related to modeling, evaluation, and practical data analysis.
institutional_requirements.academic_policies_and_institutional_stan: Adheres to university-wide policies on integrity, accessibility, and digital content use.
institutional_requirements.department_syllabus_requirements: Required components include outcomes, grading policy, and institutional statements.

prior_feedback.historical_course_evaluation_results: Historical Evaluation Results & Feedback highlights preference for hands-on learning; pacing adjustments recommended in early weeks.
